message:
Gentle myth-buster for cat lovers: purring doesn't always mean "I'm happy." 🐱💭

We tend to read that soft rumble as pure contentment, and a lot of the time it is. But cats also purr when they're frightened, in pain, or feeling unwell. Many experts believe purring can be self-soothing — a way for a cat to calm itself, and possibly even to help its body heal, since those low-frequency vibrations may support tissue and bone.

Why does this matter? Because it's a good reminder to read the WHOLE cat, not just the sound. A purring cat who's hiding, hunched, off their food, or tucked away in a quiet corner may actually be telling you something is wrong.

You know your cat best. Trust your gut, and when something feels "off," a vet visit is never a wasted trip. 💛

Did this one surprise you? 🐾
